claim: The word-initial glyph constraints {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final glyph constraints {y,n,l,r,o} are artifacts of a cipher that maps plaintext syllable-onset and syllable-coda phonemes to distinct glyph classes, not morphological or grammatical constraints, meaning that these positional biases are cipher-structural rather than language-structural.
evidence_for: Average word length 5.03 with 25 glyphs is more consistent with a syllabic or consonantal encoding than a full alphabet, where 25 letters would produce shorter words
Glyph positional constraints are unusually rigid: if they reflected grammar, we'd expect more positional diversity across 226 folios of varied content
In a syllabic cipher mapping CV or VC pairs to single glyphs, onset glyphs (C+V) would naturally cluster at word-start and coda glyphs (V+C or final consonants) at word-end
Mutual information between adjacent glyphs would be elevated compared to alphabetic text if glyphs encode syllable units rather than phonemes
evidence_against: Natural languages with alphabetic writing also show word-initial and word-final biases (e.g., Latin words rarely end in b, d, g)
With only 25 glyphs encoding syllables, the phoneme inventory would be implausibly small for Latin or Italian
H514/H517 (confidence 0.514/0.517) favor Vigenere or contact-based explanations for the same positional constraints
test: Compute pointwise mutual information (PMI) between all adjacent glyph pairs within words across the full corpus. Compare the mean and variance of within-word PMI to values computed from a shuffled corpus preserving word lengths and glyph frequencies. Then compare observed PMI distribution to PMI distributions computed from alphabetically-encoded Latin text and from syllabically-encoded Japanese (kana) text of similar length.
test_metric: Mean within-word adjacent-glyph PMI vs. shuffled baseline; KL-divergence of Voynich PMI distribution from Latin-alphabetic and Japanese-kana PMI distributions
test_threshold: If Voynich PMI distribution KL-divergence from kana is less than KL-divergence from Latin-alphabetic, this supports syllabic encoding; if mean PMI exceeds shuffled baseline by more than 2 standard deviations, positional constraints are cipher-structural rather than random

tests_run.details: Observed avg word length: 5.03. Expected: ~5.0. Difference: 0.03.

reasoning: Syllabic ciphers were used in the medieval Mediterranean and would explain the small glyph inventory, the rigid positional constraints, and the moderate Zipf fit simultaneously; PMI analysis can distinguish syllabic from alphabetic encoding without requiring decipherment by measuring the degree of glyph co-occurrence structure within word boundaries.
